content:
# Mark Chapter 8 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 36r–36v (PDF pages 71–72) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_008_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_008_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ In those days, when again a great crowd had gathered and they had nothing to eat, he called his disciples and said to them,

² "I have compassion on this crowd, because they have been with me now three days and have nothing to eat.

³ If I send them away hungry to their homes, they will collapse on the way—and some of them have come from far away."

⁴ His disciples answered him, "Where could anyone find enough bread to feed these people out here in this desolate place?"

⁵ He asked them, "How many loaves do you have?" They said, "Seven."

⁶ He directed the crowd to sit down on the ground. Taking the seven loaves, he gave thanks, broke them, and gave them to his disciples to set before the people. And they set them before the crowd.

⁷ They also had a few small fish. He blessed them and said that these also should be set before them.

⁸ They ate and were satisfied. And they took up the surplus of broken pieces—seven baskets full.

⁹ There were about four thousand people. Then he sent them away.

¹⁰ Immediately he got into the boat with his disciples and went to the district of Dalmanutha.

¹¹ The Pharisees came and began to argue with him, seeking from him a sign from heaven to test him.

¹² He sighed deeply in his spirit and said, "Why does this generation seek a sign? Truly I say to you, no sign will be given to this generation."

¹³ He left them, got into the boat again, and went to the other side.

¹⁴ Now they had forgotten to bring bread, and they had only one loaf with them in the boat.

¹⁵ He cautioned them, saying, "Watch out—beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and the leaven of Herod."

¹⁶ They began discussing with one another the fact that they had no bread.

¹⁷ Aware of this, Jesus said to them, "Why are you discussing the fact that you have no bread? Do you not yet perceive or understand? Are your hearts hardened?

¹⁸ Having eyes, do you not see? Having ears, do you not hear? And do you not remember?

¹⁹ When I broke the five loaves for the five thousand, how many baskets full of broken pieces did you take up?" They said to him, "Twelve."

²⁰ "And the seven for the four thousand, how many baskets full of broken pieces did you take up?" They said to him, "Seven."

²¹ And he said to them, "Do you not yet understand?"

²² They came to Bethsaida. And some people brought a blind man to him and begged him to touch him.

²³ He took the blind man by the hand and led him outside the village. And when he had spit on his eyes and laid his hands on him, he asked him, "Do you see anything?"

²⁴ He looked up and said, "I see people, but they look like trees walking."

²⁵ Then Jesus laid his hands on his eyes again. The man looked intently, and his sight was restored—he saw everything clearly.

²⁶ He sent him to his home, saying, "Do not even enter the village."

²⁷ Jesus went on with his disciples to the villages of Caesarea Philippi. On the way he asked his disciples, "Who do people say that I am?"

²⁸ They told him, "John the Baptist; others say Elijah; and still others, one of the prophets."

²⁹ He asked them, "But who do you say that I am?" Peter answered him, "You are the Christ."

³⁰ And he strictly charged them to tell no one about him.

³¹ Then he began to teach them that the Son of Man must suffer many things and be rejected by the elders and the chief priests and the scribes, and be killed, and after three days rise again.

³² He said this plainly, holding nothing back. And Peter took him aside and began to rebuke him.

³³ But turning and seeing his disciples, he rebuked Peter and said, "Get behind me, Satan! For you are not thinking the things of God, but the things of men."

³⁴ And calling the crowd to him with his disciples, he said to them, "If anyone would come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross and follow me.

³⁵ For whoever would save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life for my sake and the gospel's will save it.

³⁶ For what does it profit a person to gain the whole world and forfeit their soul?

³⁷ For what can a person give in return for their soul?

³⁸ For whoever is ashamed of me and of my words in this adulterous and sinful generation, of him will the Son of Man also be ashamed when he comes in the glory of his Father with the holy angels."

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

1. **v. 29 — "Christ" vs. "Messiah":** EMET and LOGOS retain "Christ" (transliterating the Greek ΧΡΙΣΤΟΣ). PNEUMA renders "Messiah" to foreground the Hebrew concept. I follow EMET and LOGOS here — "Christ" preserves the Greek text as written and is the standard rendering in formal translation. PNEUMA's choice is defensible for a dynamic translation, and her note explaining the reasoning is valuable.

2. **vv. 35-37 — ψυχή rendering:** EMET uses "life" throughout. LOGOS uses "life" in v. 35 and "soul" in vv. 36-37, noting the shift in semantic weight. PNEUMA uses "life" in v. 35 and "very self" / "yourself" in vv. 36-37. I follow LOGOS's approach — "life" where the physical/metaphorical play dominates, "soul" where the eternal dimension is primary. This captures the full range of ψυχή.

3. **v. 32 — "plainly" / "openly":** EMET: "openly he was speaking the word." LOGOS: "He said this plainly." PNEUMA: "He said all this in plain language, holding nothing back." I combined LOGOS's conciseness with PNEUMA's amplification: "He said this plainly, holding nothing back." The Greek ΠΑΡΡΗΣΙΑ carries both senses — openness and boldness.

4. **v. 33 — "Get behind me" vs. "Go behind me":** EMET renders ΥΠΑΓΕ ΟΠΙΣΩ ΜΟΥ as "Go behind me" (more literal). LOGOS and PNEUMA use "Get behind me" (more natural English). I follow the majority — "Get behind me" is the established English rendering and captures the force of the command.

5. **The two-stage healing as narrative metaphor (vv. 22-26):** All three translators note this connection. The blind man's partial sight ("people like trees walking") mirrors Peter's partial understanding (correct confession, wrong expectation). Mark places this healing deliberately between the disciples' incomprehension (vv. 14-21) and Peter's half-right confession (v. 29). The structure is Mark's theology in narrative form.

6. **v. 38 — "adulterous":** EMET and LOGOS retain "adulterous" (μοιχαλίς). PNEUMA renders "unfaithful" to preserve the covenantal metaphor. I retain "adulterous" — the prophetic resonance (Hosea, Jeremiah) is stronger with the literal term, and the metaphor of Israel as God's unfaithful spouse is well-established.

No ⚠️ flagged disagreements for this chapter.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
